deck_meta.title: The 2026 AI Index
deck_meta.subtitle: AI is scaling — the systems around it are not
deck_meta.authors: Yolanda Gil · Raymond Perrault et al.
deck_meta.org: Stanford HAI · AI Index Report 2026 (9th ed.)

slides.title:
The 2026 AI Index
Report
slides.subtitle: Capability has not plateaued · the U.S.-China gap has closed · adoption is faster than the PC era — but governance, education, and work are not keeping up.

slides.title:
From “will AI work?”
to “can systems keep up?”
slides.lede: The 9th edition of the AI Index makes the underlying question visible. AI capability is accelerating; the U.S.-China model gap has effectively closed; adoption is outpacing every prior platform. The bottleneck is no longer the technology — it is the policy, workforce, and evaluation systems around it.

from_block.label: Old narrative
points: Capability gains will slow as we approach human baselines
U.S. labs hold a comfortable lead over China
Enterprise adoption will follow a slow technology curve
Talent and capital will continue to concentrate in the U.S.

to_block.label: What 2026 actually shows
points: Capability is still accelerating — SWE-bench 60% → ~100% in a year
U.S.-China flagship gap collapsed to 2.7%
53% population reach in 3 years — faster than the PC and the internet
U.S. AI researcher net inflow fell 89% since 2017

slides.section_title:
Four headline numbers from
the 2026 edition.
preview: U.S.-China flagship model gap shrunk to 2.7%
Generative AI reached 53% of the population in 3 years
U.S. AI talent net inflow collapsed 89%

slides.title: The headline numbers.
slides.subtitle: Four data points that reframe the strategic conversation for 2026.

stats.label: SWE-bench Verified
stats.note: AI moved from 60% of human baseline to near-100% on the most serious coding benchmark in a single year. Capability is still accelerating.

stats.label: U.S.-China gap
stats.note: DeepSeek-R1 briefly matched the U.S. frontier in Feb 2025. By Mar 2026 the flagship Anthropic model leads by just 2.7% — effectively closed.

stats.label: 3-year reach
stats.note: Generative AI reached 53% of the population in 3 years — faster than the PC or the internet. Singapore 61%, UAE 54%, U.S. rank 24 (28.3%).

stats.label: U.S. AI talent inflow
stats.note: AI researchers moving to the U.S. fell 89% since 2017 — with 80% of that drop in the last year alone. The talent reshuffle is real.

slides.footer_note: Source: AI Index 2026 · Technical Performance · R&D · Public Opinion · Talent chapters

preview: AI is scaling — the systems around it are not
Model choice is no longer the decisive variable
Capability is jagged — superhuman in some tasks, sub-human in others

slides.title:
AI is scaling. The systems
around it are not.
slides.subtitle: Capability and adoption are running ahead of governance, evaluation, education, and workforce policy.
slides.primary_quote: The data does not point in a single direction. It reveals a field that is scaling faster than the systems around it can adapt.
slides.secondary_quote: AI capability is not plateauing. It is accelerating and reaching more people than ever.
slides.implication: The strategic question for enterprises and policy-makers has shifted from “can AI do this?” to “can our organisation, our regulations, and our workforce absorb this change fast enough?” The bottleneck is no longer the technology.

slides.section_title:
Two patterns the data
makes unavoidable.
preview: U.S. vs China: leadership is now multi-axis, not single-line
Productivity gains and entry-level decline are appearing together
Capability is jagged — same model, brilliant and broken

slides.title:
U.S. and China — leadership
is now multi-axis.
slides.subtitle: The single-line “who is ahead” narrative no longer fits the data. Each country leads on different dimensions.

points: Top-tier model count and high-impact patents
$285.9B private AI investment in 2025 (vs $12.4B in China)
5,427 datacenters — 10× any other country
1,953 new AI companies founded — 10× the runner-up
Leads in paper count, citations, AI patents
Leads in industrial-robot installations
DeepSeek-R1 briefly matched the U.S. frontier (Feb 2025)
23× investment gap is misleading — government funds excluded

slides.callout: Korea leads on per-capita AI patents. The interesting strategic frontier is no longer “U.S. vs China” — it is which country gets the deployment, governance, and workforce stack right first.

slides.title:
Productivity gains and
entry-level decline — together.
slides.subtitle: AI is delivering measurable productivity gains. It is also hitting the bottom rungs of the career ladder first.

points: Customer service productivity up 14–26%
Software development productivity up 14–26%
Enterprise agent deployment still in the single digits
Adoption rate is fast — ROI capture remains uneven
U.S. developers age 22–25: −20% employment since 2024
Senior developer headcount still growing
AI is eating the junior tasks first — not the senior ones
Anthropic 81k & OECD report the same pattern

slides.callout: The combination is the real story. Enterprises capture the gain; the entry-level worker carries the cost. The 5-year risk is a missing middle — no senior pipeline if junior roles vanish.

slides.section_title:
Two structural issues hiding
behind the headline progress.
preview: Capability is jagged — superhuman and sub-human in the same model
Experts and the public live in different worlds — 50pt gap on jobs
Responsible AI is falling behind capability AI

slides.title:
Brilliant and broken — in
the same model.
slides.subtitle: The two patterns the index makes unavoidable: jagged capability, and a public-expert gap that will shape policy.

cards.body: Capability is not a single ladder. The same flagship model wins gold at the International Mathematical Olympiad — and reads an analog clock correctly only 50% of the time.
cards.evidence: Gemini Deep Think — IMO gold medal; analog clock reading 50.1%. AI agents on OSWorld jumped 12% → ~66%, but still fail one task in three.
cards.implication: Product teams must build capability maps — atomic-task tests — not capability points. “It felt good in the demo” is the most common failure mode for enterprise AI agents.

cards.body: 73% of AI experts expect a positive impact on jobs. Only 23% of the public agrees. A 50-point gap on the most consequential AI question — between the people building it and the people living with it.
cards.evidence: AI incidents recorded jumped from 233 (2024) to 362 (2025). Responsible-AI benchmarks remain sparse — most frontier labs report capability metrics only.
cards.implication: Public trust is now the rate-limiting step on adoption. Enterprises that treat workforce anxiety as a comms problem will under-deploy. Treat it as design input.

slides.footer_note: Source: Technical Performance · Public Opinion · Responsible AI chapters

slides.section_title:
Five concrete moves
for enterprise leaders.
preview: Treat model choice as commodity — invest in deployment instead
Build capability maps, not capability demos
Protect the junior pipeline before it collapses

slides.title: Five moves for enterprise leaders.
slides.subtitle: Reorganise around deployment, governance, and workforce — not model selection.

actions.what: Stop spending strategy cycles on which frontier model to license. A 2.7% gap is not a moat — deployment, data, and change management are.
actions.value: Frees senior bandwidth for variables that actually move the ROI needle

actions.what: For each AI use case, decompose the workflow into atomic tasks. Test each against your evals before promising the outcome to the business.
actions.value: Counters the Jagged Frontier risk · prevents capability-point illusions

actions.what: Build AI-augmented apprenticeship paths. Use AI as a tutor for the junior role — not as a substitute for it. Track entry-level headcount as a KPI.
actions.value: Prevents the 5-year missing-middle risk · keeps your senior pipeline alive

actions.what: Adopt a responsible-AI eval stack with the same rigor as capability evals. Track AI-incident rate as an internal KPI alongside latency and accuracy.
actions.value: Closes the perception gap with regulators and the workforce · reduces incident exposure

actions.what: Run workforce-sentiment listening tied to actual exposure data. Communicate AI plans with the 50-point gap in mind — not the expert view.
actions.value: Treats trust as deployment infrastructure · turns anxiety into participation

slides.title:
AI is scaling faster than
the systems around it.
slides.subtitle: The 2026 Index reframes the strategic question for the next five years.

pillars.body: Capability did not plateau. The U.S.-China gap closed. Adoption outpaced the PC and the internet. Model choice is no longer the decisive strategic variable.
The bottleneck moved from the model to the systems around it: deployment, evaluation, education, workforce, governance. That is where the next 5 years of value will be captured — or lost.
Jagged capability and the entry-level squeeze. The public-expert perception gap. Government-fund AI investment that does not show up in private-capital data. The talent reshuffle out of the U.S.

slides.closing_line: The opportunity is not to pick the right model — it is to build the deployment, governance, and workforce stack that lets the model land.

slides.title: Thank you.
